title: Analytic (closed-form generalized chi-square) vs Monte-Carlo emergence noise null
scope: Model-relative: null residual under the non-negative cone of measured single-gene effects; not a biological claim.

norman.source: Norman 2019 combinatorial CRISPRa (file label A549; canonical K562 provenance caveat)

headline.conservative_direction: 0/289 violations: analytic null >= Monte-Carlo null on every double, both screens.
headline.certified_set_nesting: Analytic certified set is a strict subset of Monte-Carlo on both screens (Norman 34 in 40, CaRPool 68 in 76); 0 analytic-only certifications. The fast path never certifies anything the reference did not.
headline.agreement: Null means agree to within 5% on 84% (Norman) / 53% (CaRPool) of doubles; tier agreement 92% / 88%; every disagreement is analytic-stricter.
headline.anisotropy: Per-gene noise anisotropy handled exactly (generalized chi-square). Across all 289 doubles the isotropic RMS-SE approximation is small on these screens (Norman median 1.71%, max 4.21%; CaRPool max 0.61%), so anisotropy is a correctness guarantee rather than a large empirical mover here; on a synthetic fixture with atom support aligned to high-noise genes the isotropic error reaches ~11%.
headline.fragility_diagnostic: The analytic-vs-MC null gap is a certificate-fragility score driven by SNR (gap vs floor_ratio Spearman -0.53 Norman / -0.78 CaRPool), NOT by active-set churn (Spearman ~0). It is self-reporting: predictable from analytic-only floor_ratio (-0.57/-0.82), so the deterministic path flags its own borderline cases. All 14 certificates it withheld sat at MC floor_ratio in [1.90,1.97] (the 1.9x bar).

anisotropy_sensitivity.quantity: percent difference in analytic null mean between true per-gene (anisotropic) SE and an isotropic RMS-SE surrogate, per double
anisotropy_sensitivity.synthetic_note: controlled fixture with active-atom support deliberately aligned to the high-noise genes (reachability fixture); isotropic error ~11% while anisotropic form stays exact
